checks.a_analysis: The output JSON contains text that is directly traceable to the source document slides. The headings, bullet points, and body text in the provided sections (Pages 1, 2, 3, 13, 24, and 25) perfectly match the content of the corresponding slides in the PDF. There are no signs of hallucinated introductions, conclusions, or section descriptions.

No phone numbers, email addresses, or specific URLs are present in either the source document or the output JSON. The QR code on page 24 is correctly identified as an image. No contact information was fabricated.

The source PDF contains 25 slides. The JSON metadata indicates a page_count of 25. The sections provided in the output (including the placeholder for omitted sections due to token budget) represent the full sequence from Page 1 to Page 25. The content of the specific pages shown (1, 2, 3, 13, 24, 25) is complete and matches the source.

Page 13 of the source document lists 5 'Use Cases'. The output JSON represents this information as a table with 5 header cells and 5 data cells in a single row. This accurately captures all the data points from the original visual layout.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: A line-by-line comparison of Page 1, Page 2, and Page 13 shows that all text strings present in the source are present in the output. For example, all five descriptions under 'Use Cases' on Page 13 were converted in their entirety without any text loss.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text content is highly faithful to the source. The conversion correctly handled punctuation (e.g., maintaining hyphens in 'Work-in-Progress' which the raw OCR sometimes misses) and formatting (converting list symbols into structured list items). There are no meaning-altering wording changes.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the source PDF slides and compared them with the provided JSON elements. The content from each slide appears once in its respective section. There are no instances where paragraphs or list items are repeated incorrectly in the output.

The output consistently uses Heading Level 1 for the title of each slide (e.g., Slide 1, Slide 2, Slide 13, Slide 25). The hierarchy is flat and appropriate for a presentation format, ensuring that each new slide starts with a top-level heading for navigation.

On page 13 of the source, there are five vertical columns of text. The pipeline has correctly identified this as a logical table structure and rendered it as a 5-column table with a header row containing the category titles and a data row containing the descriptions. The structural mapping from the visual layout to the table format is accurate.

The reading order follows the slide-by-slide progression of the original presentation. Within each slide, the elements are ordered logically from top to bottom (title, then content, then images). For the multi-column layout on slide 13, the table columns correctly follow the left-to-right reading order of the source.

Slide 2 contains a bulleted list of agenda items. The JSON output correctly identifies this as an unordered list and preserves the five distinct items. The meaning and structure of the list are maintained.
The source document contains no visible hyperlinks. Correspondingly, no links were injected or created in the JSON output.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML document faithfully replicates the visual organization of the source PDF slides. Content from multi-column slides (such as page 13 and page 23) has been appropriately restructured into a linear flow or a table, which is an expected adaptation for responsive and accessible HTML. The information remains in the same logical order as the source.

The document contains one primary complex layout on page 13 (OneDrive Use Cases) presented as five columns. This has been correctly converted into an HTML table that is visually clear, with aligned columns and readable content. No other tables were found in the source document.

Several meaningful, content-bearing images and diagrams from the source PDF are missing in the HTML output. Specifically, the folder organization diagram (page 9), the photographic image of binders (page 14), the gears collaboration diagram (page 16), the 'DATA' blocks image (page 17), the co-authoring laptop diagram (page 19), and the QR code for follow-up (page 24) are all absent from the rendered HTML.
checks.b_evidence_source: Diagram of folders (p. 9); photo of colorful binders (p. 14); gears diagram (p. 16); 'DATA' blocks image (p. 17); laptop co-authoring diagram (p. 19); QR code image (p. 24).
checks.b_evidence_output: The corresponding sections in the HTML (Organization & Structure, Best Practices, Use Cases, Office Applications, and Follow-Up) are missing these visual elements.
checks.b_evidence_location: Sections: Organization & Structure, Best Practices (OneDrive), Use Cases (SharePoint), Best Practices (SharePoint), Office Applications, and NC DIT Innovation Summit Follow-Up.

checks.a_analysis: In the 'Collaboration' and 'Power Automate Flows' sections, sub-headings within the body content are visually duplicated. After the respective content blocks, the headings are listed again as plain text items, which does not reflect the source PDF's design and creates redundant body content.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 8: 'Collaboration' section headings appear once. Page 21: 'Power Automate Flows' section headings appear once.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML displays 'OneDrive Co-authoring', 'Advanced SharePoint Features', and 'Check-in/Check-out Options' as a list at the end of the Collaboration section, following their primary use as headers. Similarly, 'Automating Approvals', 'Custom App Development', and 'Unified Search Experience' are repeated as a list at the end of the Power Automate section.
